JSW STEEL Plans Capacity Expansion & Green Steel Targets

• 🏭 Production Update: Feb 2026 crude steel output 23.66 lakh tonnes (↓2% YoY).

• 🔧 Upgrade Impact: Output affected by Blast Furnace 3 shutdown at Vijayanagar for capacity expansion. 

• 📈 Utilization: Excluding BF3, Indian operations achieved ~97% capacity utilization with ~8% YoY volume growth.

• 🚀 Expansion Plan: Steel capacity to increase from 35.7 MTPA to ~48.9 MTPA in next 4 years.

• 🌱 Decarbonization Goal: Target 42% CO₂ reduction by 2030 and net zero emissions by 2050. 

🟢 Impact: Positive
Capacity expansion and green steel strategy support long term growth and sustainability positioning.

Morgan Stanley on India Energy

Impact of oil shocks in India has varied since price deregulation was introduced in 2014

With oil equities typically underperforming initially but then significantly outperforming in the two years after a shock

See 2026 shaping up for a similar response, and prefer Indian refiners and OMCs

As tends to happen in energy shocks, governments freeze fuel prices

2026 will likely be no different

We think policy stability will be maintained, with no windfall taxes (considering the stability laws passed by parliament in 2025)

reports.text:
FERTILIZERS STOCKS; The Iran conflict risks disrupting fertilizer production critical to global food, as the Middle East hosts major plants and raw material exporters.

About 25–35% of these raw materials pass through the Strait of Hormuz, now effectively closed. Iran, the fourth-largest urea exporter, and Saudi Arabia face supply hits, pushing Egyptian urea prices up 33%. Sulphur prices have also surged, with nearly half of global exports from the region.

Attacks on energy infrastructure, like Qatar’s gas cuts, could further threaten fertilizer supply, which CEO Svein Tore Holsether says underpins half of global food production.

reports.text:
Househelp Apps Cross 2 Million Monthly Orders but Profitability Concerns Grow

India’s instant househelp platforms like Urban Company, Snabbit, and Pronto have crossed 2 million monthly orders in February, up 45–50% from about 1.3 million in December.

Urban Company leads with 840,000–850,000 orders, followed by Snabbit (~830,000) and Pronto (~340,000).

Market share in February:
Urban Company: 49.2%
Snabbit: 36.4%
Pronto: 14.3%

Companies are expanding aggressively into new cities and micro-markets, increasing scale in the 10-minute househelp services segment.

To acquire users, platforms are offering deep discounts, with services priced as low as ₹1 per hour in some markets.

The sector is burning significant cash, with combined spending of about $10–11 million in February.

Investors are increasingly questioning whether the business model can achieve sustainable profitability.

Urban Company’s stock has fallen over 40% since listing, reflecting concerns over unit economics.

D2C Saree Startups Ride Fashion Shift Wave

Market Opportunity  
- ~54 online-first saree brands emerge in massive ₹80,000 Cr market.  
- Gen Z/millennials drive lighter, contemporary styling demand.  

Key Players  
- Suta, Sudathi, Mrida, Hassiyn, Kalki Fashion, Taneira, Kathia lead.  
- Sarees evolve from ceremonial to daily fashion statements.  

Product Innovation  
- Pre-draped/"one-minute" sarees boost accessibility.  
- Pricing ₹699-₹4,999 widens mass premium appeal.  

Funding Momentum  
- $25 Mn raised across 10 rounds in 2024-25.  
- Backers: Accel, Blume, 3 Peaks Ventures.  

Channel Strength  
- Instagram/WhatsApp social commerce dominates.  
- Repeat buys fuel sustainable growth.  

Key Takeaway:  
54 D2C saree brands tap ₹80k Cr market via modern styling/pre-draped innovation—social commerce + funding accelerates Gen Z disruption.

Cement Margins Squeezed by Fuel Spike

Fuel Cost Jump  
- Petcoke +11% MoM to ~$135/t; imported coal +7% to ~$110/t.  
- West Asia tensions drive alternative fuel inflation.  

Margin Lag Effect  
- 30-45 day inventories delay immediate hit.  
- Q4 impact surfaces if prices stay elevated.  

Price Hike Response  
- ₹10/bag hikes eyed across trade/non-trade.  
- Feb saw ₹5-10 regional increases; March muted so far.  

Demand Backdrop  
- Infra spending sustains positive volume outlook.  
- Construction activity cushions pricing power.  

Steel Parallel  
- Coal/scrap/freight/LNG costs rise with disruptions.  
- Global logistics issues test steel price transmission.  

Key Takeaway:  
Petcoke/coal spikes threaten cement margins from Q4—₹10/bag hikes critical vs steady infra demand support.

Durables & Auto Face April Price Hikes on Conflict Costs

Input Cost Surge  
- Metals/plastics/crude derivatives spike from West Asia war.  
- Copper/Al up ~30% in 2 months; steel +12-15%.  

Crude Impact  
- Oil crosses $90/bbl, eyes $100; hits petrochemicals (PP/polymers).  
- Weak rupee amplifies imported input inflation.  

Supply Disruptions  
- Chip shortages + freight delays from conflict routes.  
- Electronics/appliance makers face compounded pressures.  

Price Actions  
- Maruti/Tata/Hyundai/Honda monitor post-prior hikes.  
- Durables (ACs/fridges) eye 5-6% increases in April.  

Mitigation Efforts  
- Hedging, localisation, supplier renegotiations underway.  
- Companies battle to limit full cost pass-through.  

Key Takeaway:  
$90+ crude + 30% metal spikes force 5-6% durables/auto hikes in April—localisation race intensifies vs conflict volatility.

reports.text:
India consumes ~35–36 million tonnes of urea annually, far above domestic production capacity of roughly 28–29 mn tonnes. This is what forces regular imports even though India is already one of the world’s largest producers of urea.

Interesting thing is that what looks like a fertilizer import problem is partly a policy incentive problem. A simple framing would be to say that demand expanded along the cheapest input rather than the most agronomically balanced one.

Much of this fertilizer demand is policy engineered tbh. Since 1977, urea prices have been tightly controlled while phosphatic & potassic fertilizers gradually moved to nutrient based pricing. Urea stayed artificially cheap which made nitrogen the default input for farmers.

 Ideal N:P:K ratio is ~4:2:1, but our farmers’ usage is almost around 7–8:3:1. Classic price response mechanism due to subsidised pricing, fields receive more urea than agronomy actually recommends. Good case of price driven consumption abuse.

India’s fertilizer imports are insanely concentrated. ~ 63% of nitrogen fertilizer imports come from Gulf producers (Saudi, Oman, Qatar, UAE). For DAP, the Gulf accounts for ~32%. Huge pc of India’s food production runs through this fertilizer corridor.

Oil Is Not Just Fuel. It Is the Base of the Industrial Economy

Oil and gas become feedstocks like naphtha, ethane and propane.

These are turned into basic chemicals such as ethylene, propylene and methanol.

Which then produce:

• Plastics
• Synthetic rubber
• Resins and foams
• Industrial chemicals

Airtel’s Nxtra Plans $1 Billion Fundraise to Expand Data Centres

Bharti Airtel’s data centre arm Nxtra plans to raise $1 billion to scale up capacity across India.

Alpha Wave Global will invest $400 million, while Bharti Airtel and Carlyle will invest $300 million each.

The company currently operates 14 core data centres and over 120 edge data centres across India.

Nxtra targets 10 GW additional capacity, compared with its current 1.4 GW capacity.

The company currently holds around 12% market share and aims to reach 25% market share within 3–4 years.

Global hyperscalers and Indian conglomerates are expected to invest over $270 billion in data centre infrastructure over the next 5–7 years.

reports.text:
**Investor Return Formula: Mental Model of Great Investors**

1. Return = Earnings Growth + P/E Expansion

2. Retail investors miss the 2nd part

3. Infosys (2000), Trent (2024), Eternal (2025): High Starting PE ➝ No PE Expansion ➝ No Return

P/E Re-rating Powers Investor Returns:

Infosys 2000: The Dotcom Bubble

a. In March 2000, Infosys stock traded at a P/E of 200. Investors priced in decades of future growth.

b. 2000 to 2009: Infosys profits grew 20X in 10 yrs (₹286 cr in 2000 to ₹5,819 cr in 2009). Very few companies in the world achieve that growth.

c. Investor Returns from 2000 to 2009: Flat (Zero returns). Why? Because the valuation multiple (P/E) normalized from 200X to 25X (87% decline).

Lessons for Investors

a. P/E multiples normalize over time because growth normalizes over time. New competitors enter, new technologies happen, founders get rich and lazy, consumer preferences change.

b. For example, you may be euphoric today about Eternal’s growth. But over the next 10 years, you may no longer be so euphoric. If their growth normalizes, you could end up with zero returns over 10 years.

c. Retail investors ignore future P/E normalization because that is the hard part to calculate. Legendary investors only focus on that part.

d. P/E re-rating changes everything. If starting P/E is low, and earnings growth improves over time, long-term investor returns can be extraordinary. If the opposite happens, long-term returns will be poor.

When Does a Stock Deserve High P/E?

a. If a company can continue to re-invest its profits at high returns for a long time-period, only then a high P/E multiple is justified. Three conditions must be met:

(1) High return on capital employed (high ROCE)

(2) High re-investment rate (large opportunity to re-invest a major portion of annual profits)

(3) Long duration of annual re-investment cycle (long runway)

b. Example: The following business performance deserves a high P/E multiple:

ROCE = 25%
Re-investment Rate = 60%
Earnings Growth =
ROCE x Reinvestment Rate
25% x 60% = 15% CAGR
Growth Duration: 20 yrs

This is 15% YoY profit growth without any need for external capital (debt or equity dilution). Just 60% of annual profits are re-invested year after year.

c. The critical factor in the above example is how long the re-investment cycle can last? Will it last for 5 years or 20 years? The compounding power of 20 years is extraordinary. But if it lasts only 5 years, and you paid high starting P/E, capital destruction will happen. (Example: Trent)

How Do 100-Bagger Returns Happen: Low Starting P/E, Steady Growth, Long Runway

a. In 2002, Titan’s P/E ratio was 8 or 10 because future growth expectations (which determine P/E multiples) were modest.

b. Titan’s branded jewellery business did not enjoy an explosive growth rate of 25% or 30% YoY. Instead, there was a reasonable growth for a long period. Brand trust increased gradually, store expansion happened steadily.

c. P/E Re-rating: Once the market recognized Titan’s long runway, P/E multiple changed from 10 to 60~80. In other words, investors were willing to pay 8X more for the same stock.

d. MATH: Assume these hypothetical figures for Titan in 2002:

EPS = ₹1
P/E = 10
Stock Price = ₹10

Earnings (EPS) Growth:
15% CAGR x 20 yrs

EPS after 20 yrs = ₹16.37

P/E after 20 yrs = 70

Stock Price = EPS x PE
= ₹16.37 x 70 = ₹1,146

Price Change in 20 Yrs:
₹10 to ₹1,146 = 114X

How did 114X returns happen?

Earnings Growth x PE Expansion

16.37 x 70

Market rewarded shareholders with a massive P/E expansion of 7X because of steady growth, long runway, and low starting P/E.

ENDQUOTE

“In the short-run, the market is a voting machine. (You can vote even for a fool based on narratives.) In the long-run, it is a weighing machine. (Scientific measurement based on unemotional facts.)” – Warren Buffett’s Guru Benjamin Graham, “The Intelligent Investor,” 1949

Credit Vikas vij

reports.text:
Macquarie: Farm Loan Waivers Hurt Banks

Maharashtra Waiver Details  
- Up to Rs 2 lakh for farmers with outstanding crop loans as of end-September 2025.  
- Announced in state budget, reviving waiver trend.  

Credit Culture Risks  
- Waivers erode credit discipline; agri NPLs expected to spike.  
- Trend of declining agri NPLs over years now reversed—negative development.  

Banking Sector Impact  
- PSU banks hit harder than private peers due to vulnerable customer base.  
- All banks must hit priority sector/agri lending targets.  

NPL Disparity  
- Private banks maintain lower agri NPLs.  
- HDFC Bank: 3.8% (FY25); SBI: 8.4% (FY25).  

Key Takeaway:  
Farm waivers threaten agri asset quality, disproportionately pressuring PSU banks—watch for rising NPLs and margin compression ahead.

Iran War Escalation: Market Chaos & Stock Plays

War Highlights (Day 9)  
- Israel hits Tehran oil depots; black smoke over city, burning oil in sewers.  
- Iran strikes Bahrain desalination plant (75% of freshwater source); UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi face drones/missiles.  
- US/Israel eye special forces for Iran's 450kg 60% enriched uranium; Trump warns of nuclear risk.  
- CENTCOM: 3,000+ Iranian targets hit, air force destroyed; Iran retains missiles/drones.  
- USS Gerald Ford, French warships deploy; Hormuz risks rise.  

Market Fallout  
- Brent Crude blasts past $100/bbl (up 15-18%), Shanghai crude/fuel up 17% limit.  
- GIFT Nifty down 700+ pts; Dow futures -900 pts, Nasdaq -1.7%, Nikkei -5-7%.  
- Gold down 2%, silver 4%; Rupee at record lows, 10Y yields up.  
- FII shorts at 87%; AI sell-off, risk-off across assets; $3T+ market wipeout.  

Positives  
- ONGC, Oil, HOEC, Vedanta: Crude surge lifts upstream.  
- HAL, BEL: Defence sentiment boost.  
- Deep Industries, Jindal Drilling: Offshore positive.  
- GE Shipping, SCI: Baltic Dry Index rise.  

Negatives  
- GAIL, Petronet LNG, CGDs, Fertilizers: LNG spike.  
- OMCs, Paints, Tyres: Higher crude costs.  
- InterGlobe Aviation: ATF hike likely.  
- L&T, VA Tech Wabag, BLS Intl, KRBL: Middle East revenue exposure.  
- MakeMyTrip, Ixigo, Thomas Cook: Travel sentiment drag.  
- Aarti Inds, Vinati Org, Atul, Vishnu Chem: Pricing shocks.  
- Hind Zinc, Gold Financiers: Precious metals fall 2-3%.  

Key Takeaway:  
Crude at $100+ fuels upstream/defence winners amid gap-down opens, but LNG/OMCs/Mideast-exposed stocks brace for pain—watch Hormuz for next leg.

Middle East War: Sector Impacts Decoded

Oil & Gas Winners  
- ONGC, Oil, HOEC, Vedanta: Brent Crude surges past $100/bbl, boosting upstream profitability.  

Defence Boost  
- HAL, BEL: Geopolitical tensions lift defence sentiment.  
- Deep Industries, Jindal Drilling: Positive sentiment from heightened offshore activity.  

Shipping Tailwinds  
- GE Shipping, SCI: Baltic Dry Index rise supports freight rates and volumes.  

LNG & Downstream Losers  
- GAIL, Petronet LNG: Spiking LNG prices erode margins.  
- CGDs, Fertilizer Companies: Higher LNG input costs squeeze profitability.  

Crude Price Hit  
- OMCs, Paints, Tyres: Elevated crude raises raw material costs.  
- InterGlobe Aviation: Likely ATF price hikes pressure aviation economics.  

Middle East Exposure Risks  
- L&T, VA Tech Wabag, BLS International, KRBL: Higher revenue dependence on the region faces disruption risks.  

Travel Sentiment Drag  
- MakeMyTrip, Ixigo, Thomas Cook: Negative sentiment amid regional instability.  

Specialty Chemicals Pressure  
- Aarti Industries, Vinati Organics, Atul, Vishnu Chemicals: Expected pricing shocks from supply chain volatility.  

Metals Decline  
- Hind Zinc, Gold Financiers: Gold and silver prices drop 2-3%.  

Key Takeaway:  
Oil explorers and defence plays gain from crude spike and sentiment, while LNG importers, OMCs, and Middle East-exposed names face near-term headwinds.

CITI ON OIL & GAS

RIL relatively better positioned amid current uncertainties

ONGC may benefit at the standalone level from higher crude prices.; gains may dilute at the consolidated level

OMCs face the highest downside risks

City gas distributors remain vulnerable to margin pressures

GAIL and Petronet LNG fall in the mid-impact category

Prolonged conflict could trigger further government intervention

Possible windfall taxes may be imposed on crude producers. Export curbs or duties on refined petroleum products are possible

Gas supply reallocation could be considered to manage shortages

Government may provide explicit budgetary support to oil marketing companies

Domestic LPG prices increased by ₹60 per cylinder recently

🚨 The dream of every Indian for the past 30 years, the Kaveri engine project. 

DRDO Chairman Samir V. Kamat said that the Kaveri engine has not delivered the thrust that is required for the LCA Tejas. The engine is working very well; it has given us a thrust of 72 kN, but the LCA needs a thrust of 83-85 kN. 🥲 

However, according to the latest info, the Kaveri engine afterburner test success reportedly displayed increased thrust of 81–83 kN. This happened after the GTRE made significant technological changes. 👌

The air mass flow of Kaveri is higher at 78 kg/s vs. 65 kg/s of the M88-2 engine developed by France. The only drawback of Kaveri is its weight, which was around 300 kg extra compared to other rivals, and now the DRDO has reportedly reduced 100 kg in the latest upgrades. This means the gap is now around 200 kg.

Critics believe that if things go as planned, HAL’s dependence on American firm GE for engines may end soon, making India self-reliant for Tejas LCA engines by the end of 2027. HOPEFULLY 🤞

PhysicsWallah – Some insights based on concalls Q3

Business Strengths
• Zero CAC model: Free YouTube content drives paid users → very low marketing cost (~8–9%).
• Massive distribution: 250+ YouTube channels, ~3.4M daily active users.
• High engagement: Students spend ~106 minutes daily on the app.
• Strong growth: Revenue growing ~30–35% annually.
• Strong cash: ~₹5,000 Cr treasury after IPO for expansion and acquisitions.

Growth Drivers
• K-12 expansion: Much larger market than test prep; could become biggest segment in 5–7 years.
• Offline expansion: 300+ centers with plans to add more.
• New exam categories: CLAT, NEET PG, IIT JAM, state boards etc.
• Regional language courses: Expanding into southern and vernacular markets.
• AI integration: Proprietary AI models (Aryabhata) for personalized learning.

Key Strategic Advantage
• Community-led growth: YouTube → App → Paid batches funnel is hard for competitors to replicate.

Major Risks
• Dependence on test-prep: JEE/NEET growth slowing in mature regions.
• Execution risk: K-12 schools, AI products, and acquisitions are new businesses.
• High investment phase: Expansion could pressure margins in near term.
• Competition: Global AI/edtech platforms entering education.
• Regulation risk: Coaching and school sectors may face policy changes.

Overall Conclusion
• Market is valuing PW not as a coaching company but as a long-term education platform.
• Future success depends on scaling K-12 + AI while maintaining strong growth in core test-prep.

Jio IPO Delayed Amid Regulatory Uncertainty

The IPO of Reliance Jio Platforms has been delayed due to pending government approvals on listing rule changes.

Reliance is waiting for the government to finalise regulatory amendments proposed by SEBI before filing its draft IPO prospectus.

SEBI earlier approved changes allowing companies with post-issue market capitalisation above ₹5 lakh crore to dilute only 2.5% stake in an IPO, instead of the current 5% requirement.

The rule change could facilitate mega listings like Jio and NSE, but it still awaits final government notification.

Reliance was targeting a Jio listing in early 2026, but the timeline may shift depending on regulatory clarity.

Investment bankers estimate the IPO could value Jio Platforms at around $170 billion.

The listing would be the first IPO of a major Reliance unit in nearly two decades and could become India’s biggest-ever IPO.

Jio has attracted over $10 billion in investments from global players like Meta and Alphabet since 2020.

AI Data Centres Don't Need Wires & Cables Now

Nvidia is moving AI data centers from copper cables wires to optics… here is why it could have repercussions for growth of Indian Wires & Cables players like Polycab, KEI etc.

Context 
Jensen Huang’s Nvidia recently committed $4B to optical ecosystem players like Coherent Corp. and Lumentum Holdings to secure silicon photonics, lasers and optical networking for next-gen AI data centers.

Why? Because copper cables are hitting limits in dense AI GPU clusters:
• Too much heat & power consumption
• Bandwidth constraints
• Physical bulk blocking airflow in racks

So hyperscale AI clusters are increasingly shifting to optical interconnects (fiber + photonics) instead of copper.

Meanwhile in India, Polycab, KEI & the entire cables & wires sector is being pitched as a pure "AI & Data Centre proxy."

KEI Industries CMD Anil Gupta called data centres a "₹25,000 crore opportunity for the cables industry" by 2030-31.

Motilal Oswal's sector note projects Indian C&W companies growing at 17% revenue CAGR over FY26–28. Key drivers cited: real estate, renewables, infra, industrial capex.

Data centres? A "powerful, long-duration driver" — but nowhere close to being the primary revenue engine today.

Even if all that plays out, the absolute cable demand from data centers remains relatively small compared to overall industry demand from housing, power, infrastructure etc.

So the real question:

Is the market overestimating the “AI/Data Center” opportunity for Indian Wires & Cables — especially when AI infrastructure itself is moving toward optics rather than copper

**The NAV Trap: Why Your “Same Day” Mutual Fund Order May Fail**

**The Reality Behind 2 PM Cutoff**
• Many investors assume placing an order before **2:00 PM** guarantees the **same day NAV**
• In reality, clicking **“Pay” at 1:45 PM** does **not guarantee** today’s NAV
• The key factor is **when the money is actually received by the Clearing Corporation**
• If funds reach after the cutoff, investors get **next day NAV**

**What Actually Happens in the System**
• Payments go through **aggregators** like **BillDesk** or **Razorpay**
• These platforms process and forward the transaction to **ICCL (Indian Clearing Corporation Ltd)**
• Even if payment is instant, **reporting delay can happen**
• Example case
• Payment completed at **13:38:13**
• Reported to clearing system at **14:32:31**
• Result: **Next Day NAV Allotment**

**Important Rule Investors Must Understand**
• NAV allotment depends on **fund realization**
• This means the time when **AMC actually receives usable funds**
• If realization happens after **2:00 PM**, order shifts to **next day NAV**
• Execution time and realization time are **two different things**

**How Smart Investors Avoid This Trap**
**Buffer Time Strategy**
• Place orders **before 1:00 PM**
• This gives buffer for **payment gateway delays**
• Helps secure **same day NAV**

**Choose the Right Payment Mode**
• Prefer **UPI or Net Banking**
• Best reliability from major banks
• **HDFC Bank**
• **ICICI Bank**
• **SBI**
• **Axis Bank**
• **Kotak Bank**
• These usually process funds **faster to clearing systems**

**Payment Modes to Avoid for Same-Day NAV**
• **NEFT**
• **RTGS**
• **Mandates / Auto debit**
• These methods may take **T+1 or even T+2** to reflect

**Investor Insight: Market Timing Mistake**
• Many investors wait for **last minute market dips**
• Large number of transactions create **payment congestion**
• Aggregator delays increase during **high volume periods**
• Late orders often lead to **unexpected next day NAV**

**Key Takeaway for Investors**
• Do not rely on the **2 PM cutoff myth**
• The real cutoff is **fund realization time**
• Always place orders **well before 1 PM**
• Early execution ensures **NAV certainty and better investment discipline**

**Global Oil Chokepoints 🛢️**

​Nearly **92% of global maritime oil** passes through just a few narrow gateways. The **Strait of Malacca** and **Strait of Hormuz** are the heavyweights, together handling over 55% of the world’s sea-traded oil.

​From the Suez Canal to the Cape of Good Hope, these routes are the arteries of the global economy. Any disruption at these "chokepoints" doesn't just stall ships—it risks global energy security and sends prices skyrocketing.

**The Sweet Spot of Iranian Crude Oil**

Iranian Light crude, with its 33-36° API gravity and 1.36-1.5% sulfur content, hits the perfect balance for global refineries. It yields high-value products like gasoline and diesel with minimal processing—unlike heavier Venezuelan Merey (16° API) or lighter US WTI (39-40° API). Despite sanctions, demand persists via Dubai networks, as refineries optimized for it struggle with substitutes. Amid 2026 Strait of Hormuz tensions, prices have spiked to $82 per barrel, highlighting its enduring appeal.

**Flexi Cap Funds: Active vs Patient Portfolios**

**Churn Spectrum**  
Flexi Cap funds vary sharply in portfolio turnover. High churners like Quant Flexi Cap trade aggressively for alpha. Patient players like Kotak Flexi Cap and PPFAS Flexi Cap hold conviction bets longer.  

**Key Examples**  
- **Quant Flexi Cap**: High turnover style; dynamic shifts across market caps for momentum plays.  
- **Kotak Flexi Cap**: Low turnover (~9%); 59 stocks, large/mid bias. Top holdings: HDFC Bank, BEL.  
- **PPFAS Flexi Cap**: Equity turnover ~14%; focuses long-term compounding with global diversification.  

**Investor Implications**  
High churn boosts short-term returns but racks up costs/taxes. Low turnover favors tax efficiency, lower expenses, steady growth. Category average exceeds 200%.  

**Key Takeaway**  
Match fund style to your horizon—aggressive for tactical edges, patient for buy-and-hold wealth building.

**Nuvama IT Sector: Gen AI Update**

Gen AI mirrors RIMS/digital waves; Indian IT partners platforms (OpenAI, Microsoft, AWS) rather than displaced.

- Greenfield: Aggressive on AI-native stacks; now in RFPs/renewals.  

**Pricing & Productivity**  
- AI work +30-40% billing premium vs legacy.  
- Efficiency gains 15-18% avg (up to 35%); token costs drive opex preference.  

**Workforce Shift**  
Gradual reskilling to AI engineers/specialists; billing includes AI agents.  
Agents shrink teams/RCA time (e.g., cyber: 5-6 eng → smaller with AI).  

**Sector Dynamics**  
Regulated sectors (insurance/healthcare) slower due to compliance.  
TAM expands long-term; Indian IT strengthens despite near-term volatility.  

**Key Takeaway:**  
Gen AI boosts IT services pricing/productivity; Indian firms well-positioned as integration partners—near-term demand choppy but structural positive.

For companies above ₹1 lakh crore, the median drawdown was 32% on April 7th 2025. That has since narrowed to below 20%. Similar patterns hold as you move down through the ₹50,000 crore and ₹10,000 crore buckets - the pain peaked and has been receding since 7th April 2025.

But what about below ₹3,000 crore mcap segment?

There has been no recovery. In fact, since April 7th, these stocks have fallen another 3-5%. The median drawdown in the sub-₹3,000 crore universe now sits at around 60% from peak. While the rest of the market has been healing, the segment where we operate has been bleeding further.

**UBS Global Research – India Banks Energy Shock Scenario (5 Mar 2026)**

**Macro Impact – Oil Price Shock**

• UBS analysed impact of rising oil prices due to **Middle East conflict**
• **+10% oil price rise → ~30 bps CPI increase**
• **GDP growth may decline ~15 bps**

• During **Russia–Ukraine war (2022)**
• Oil jumped **$10–30 per barrel for ~6 months**
• Inflation rose **~80–90 bps**
• RBI raised **Repo Rate ~150 bps**

**Scenario 1 – Oil Price Rise < $10 per Barrel**

• **EPS Impact: +1% to −3% (Limited impact)**

• **Loan Growth:** Stable due to **higher SME working capital demand**
• **NIM:** Stable as **rate cut cycle may end**
• **Non-interest income:** Slight decline due to **treasury losses**
• **Credit costs:** **Minimal asset quality impact**

• **Preferred Banks:**
• **Axis Bank**
• **Kotak Mahindra Bank**

**Scenario 2 – Oil Price Rise $20–30 per Barrel**

• **EPS Impact: −4% to −8% (Sector pressure)**

• **Loan Growth:** Slight slowdown due to **consumption weakness**
• **NIM:** **0 to −5 bps decline** due to loan mix change
• **Non-interest income:** Decline due to **lower consumption and treasury losses**
• **Credit costs:** **0 to +5 bps increase**

• **Preferred Bank:** **ICICI Bank**

**Banks vs NBFCs**

• UBS prefers **Banks over NBFCs** in oil shock scenario
• Banks have **stronger balance sheets and lower funding risk**

**UBS Preferred Banking Picks**

• **Axis Bank – 1.7x FY27E PBV**
• **Kotak Mahindra Bank – 1.6x FY27E PBV**

**Other Buy Rated Banks**

• **ICICI Bank – 2.2x PBV**
• **HDFC Bank – 1.8x PBV**
• **Bank of Baroda – 1.0x PBV**
• **Canara Bank – 1.0x PBV**

**EPS Sensitivity to Macro Risks**

• **Loan Growth −1 ppt → EPS impact −0.1% to −1.7%**
• **NIM −5 bps → EPS impact −1.3% to −3.8%**
• **Non-interest income −2% → EPS impact −0.9% to −3.8%**
• **Credit cost +5 bps → EPS impact −1.1% to −2.8%**

**Most Resilient Banks**

• **ICICI Bank (EPS impact ~−3.9%)**
• **Kotak Mahindra Bank (~−4.8%)**
• **Axis Bank (~−5.0%)**

**More Sensitive Banks**

• **IDFC First Bank (~−8.3%)**
• **Bandhan Bank (~−7.9%)**
• **IndusInd Bank (~−7.8%)**

**Key Takeaway for Investors**

• **Mild oil shock → Limited banking sector impact**
• **Sharp oil spike → Consumption slowdown & margin pressure**
• **Large private banks remain structurally stronger**

**Strategy**

• **Axis Bank & Kotak Bank for moderate oil risk**
• **ICICI Bank for high oil shock scenario**
• **Private Banks likely to outperform PSU Banks & NBFCs**

Maruti Suzuki to Expand Capacity to Meet Rising Demand

Maruti Suzuki plans to increase annual production capacity by 500,000 units from next fiscal year to meet strong domestic demand.

The company currently has installed capacity of about 2.6 million units per year across plants in Haryana and Gujarat.

New plants will add ~250,000 units annually each, with capacity additions happening in phases.

The Kharkhoda (Haryana) plant is expected to start production in Q1 of next fiscal, while the fourth line at Hansalpur (Gujarat) may begin in Q2.

Maruti currently has a large order backlog of around 200,000 vehicles.

Dealer inventory is tight at ~12 days, significantly lower than the ideal level of about 30 days.

The company produces 220,000–230,000 vehicles per month, with 170,000–180,000 units for domestic sales and ~50,000 units for exports.

Exports to Africa, the Middle East and other markets are also expected to grow with higher production capacity.

MRF to Invest ₹5,300 Cr in New Tyre Plant in Tamil Nadu

MRF plans to set up a greenfield tyre manufacturing plant in Tamil Nadu with an investment of ~₹5,300 crore.

The plant will be located at Sipcot Industrial Park in Sivaganga district.

The project will manufacture automotive tyres and related products.

The investment will be implemented over ~12 years and is expected to create around 1,000 direct jobs.

The company has signed a non-binding MoU with the Tamil Nadu government through its investment facilitation agency.

MRF currently operates four manufacturing units in India across Goa, Telangana, and Gujarat.

The project is subject to land allocation, infrastructure support, and regulatory approvals from the state government.

**METALS & CEMENT | HSBC VIEW**  

**Middle East Supply Impact**  
- **Cement:** Cost inflation from **higher pet coke prices** due to coal price surge.  
- **Coal:** No direct impact on Indian supplies.  
- **Aluminum:** No impact on India; **most vulnerable** if disruptions persist — alumina prices may fall.  
- **Steel:** Some disruption expected; **India unaffected** directly.  

**European Ripple Effects**  
- **European steel costs** to rise from elevated gas prices.  

**Cement Pricing Need**  
- Prices require **4-5% hike** in **April-May** to offset energy cost inflation.  

**Key Takeaway:**  
Cement margins face immediate pet coke pressure requiring April-May price hikes; aluminum carries highest disruption risk while Indian steel/coal relatively insulated.
